w:r.w:t: Database • Authentication • API Endpoints • Security • Deployment
age field added to user_profiles table
Every task in this document follows the same format:
Explains why this task matters — read this before starting
Exact, ordered steps to complete the task — follow them top to bottom
Copy these exactly — code that needs to go in a file
What you should see when the step works correctly
TIP / NOTE / IMPORTANT / COMMON MISTAKE boxes
The checklist item — tick this off when complete
Never skip a task and jump ahead. Each task builds on the previous one. If something is not working, do not move forward — fix it first, or ask your team for help.
Keep a terminal window open at all times. Most steps require you to type commands. On Windows, use PowerShell or Git Bash. On Mac/Linux, use Terminal.
Phase 0: Set Up Your Development Environment
Before you write any project code, your computer needs the right software installed. This phase takes about 1–2 hours. Do it before the hackathon.
Install Node.js (the JavaScript runtime for your backend)
Your backend code runs on Node.js. Without it, you cannot run any JavaScript outside of a browser. npm (Node Package Manager) comes with Node.js and is how you install libraries.
Go to https://nodejs.org — download the LTS version (the one that says 'Recommended For Most Users').
Run the installer. Accept all defaults. It will install both Node.js and npm.
Open a new terminal window and type these two commands:
✓ Expected result: You should see version numbers like v20.x.x and 10.x.x. If you see 'command not found', restart your computer and try again.
DONE WHEN: Running node --version shows a version number (v18 or higher).
Install Docker Desktop (runs your database and Redis in containers)

Instead of installing PostgreSQL and Redis directly on your computer (which is complicated), we use Docker. Docker runs them in isolated 'containers' — like mini virtual machines. This keeps your computer clean and ensures everyone on the team has identical setups.
Go to https://docker.com/products/docker-desktop — download for your operating system (Windows or Mac).
Run the installer. After installation, open Docker Desktop. Wait for it to say 'Docker Desktop is running' in the bottom left corner.
✓ Expected result: You should see version numbers. If Docker Desktop is not running, these commands will fail.
DONE WHEN: Running docker --version shows a version number and Docker Desktop shows the green 'running' indicator.
Install a database viewer (TablePlus — to see your database visually)
You will be writing SQL and creating tables. A database viewer lets you see your tables and data like a spreadsheet, which is essential for debugging. TablePlus is free and beginner-friendly.
Go to https://tableplus.com — download the free version for your OS.
Run the installer. You do not need to open it yet — you will connect it to your database in Phase 1.
DONE WHEN: TablePlus is installed and appears in your applications.
Install VS Code and the essential extensions
VS Code is the code editor you will write all your backend code in. Extensions add features like automatic code formatting and error highlighting that save beginners a lot of time.
Go to https://code.visualstudio.com — download and install.
Open VS Code, click the Extensions icon on the left sidebar (looks like building blocks), and install each of these:
REST Client (by Huachao Mao) — for testing your API
Thunder Client (by Ranga Vadhineni) — alternative API tester
Step 3: Set Prettier as default formatter
Press Ctrl+Shift+P (Cmd+Shift+P on Mac), type 'Format Document With', select Prettier. Then go to File > Preferences > Settings, search 'format on save', and tick the checkbox.
DONE WHEN: VS Code opens, and you can see your installed extensions in the sidebar.
Set up the backend project folder structure
Every file in your backend needs to be in the right place. A well-organized project is much easier to debug and build on. This structure is the standard for Express.js applications.
Step 1: Clone the shared repo and navigate to the backend folder
This creates a package.json file that tracks your project and its dependencies:
Create all folders now — this is your project map:
mkdir config routes services middleware db scripts logs
Step 4: Create the main entry file
Create a file called server.js in the backend root — this is where your server starts:
Run ls (Mac/Linux) or dir (Windows) to confirm you see:
config/ routes/ services/ middleware/ db/ scripts/ logs/ server.js package.json
DONE WHEN: Running ls inside the backend folder shows all the above folders and files.
These are the libraries your backend depends on. Each one solves a specific problem so you do not have to write everything from scratch.
These are packages your server needs when running:
npm install express dotenv cors helmet morgan express-rate-limit express-validator
npm install pg ioredis bcryptjs jsonwebtoken multer sharp cloudinary
These are only used during development (not in production):
npm install --save-dev nodemon jest supertest eslint prettier
Open package.json — you should see all packages listed under 'dependencies' and 'devDependencies'.
✓ Expected result: The node_modules folder is created and package.json lists all installed packages.
DONE WHEN: Running npm install inside the backend folder completes without errors.
Never commit the node_modules folder to Git. Create a .gitignore file in your backend folder and add node_modules/ as the first line.
Your backend needs secret values like API keys and database passwords. These must NEVER be put directly in your code or committed to GitHub. The .env file holds them locally on your machine only.
Open the file in VS Code and paste this content (your team will fill in the real values):
# JWT Secrets (make these long random strings — 32+ characters)
Make sure .env is in your .gitignore file. Create .gitignore if it does not exist and add:
DONE WHEN: The .env file exists in the backend folder and is listed in .gitignore.
If you accidentally commit your .env file to GitHub with real API keys inside, those keys must be immediately rotated/regenerated. This is a serious security issue.

The database is the heart of your backend. It stores every piece of data — users, profiles, wardrobe items, outfits, everything. This phase creates all the database tables and a Docker environment to run PostgreSQL locally.
Write the Docker Compose file (starts PostgreSQL + Redis with one command)

Docker Compose lets you define and start multiple services (PostgreSQL database and Redis cache) with a single command. Without this, everyone on the team would need to install and configure databases differently on their own machines.
In your backend root folder, create a file called docker-compose.yml and add this content exactly:
Run this command in the backend folder — Docker Desktop must be running:
Step 3: Verify both containers are running
✓ Expected result: You should see both styleai_postgres and styleai_redis listed with status 'running'.
DONE WHEN: docker compose ps shows both containers with status 'running'.
The -d flag means 'detached mode' — the containers run in the background. To stop them: docker compose down. To see their logs: docker compose logs postgres.
Connect TablePlus to your local PostgreSQL database
TablePlus gives you a visual view of your database so you can inspect tables, run queries, and verify your migrations worked correctly. This is essential for debugging.
Step 1: Open TablePlus and create a new connection
Step 2: Fill in the connection details
Click 'Test' at the bottom of the connection dialog.
✓ Expected result: You should see a green checkmark and the message 'Connected'.
Step 4: Save and open the connection
Click 'Save', then double-click the connection to open it. You should see an empty database with no tables yet.
DONE WHEN: TablePlus opens a connection to styleai_db and shows the database (empty at this point).
Set up the database connection in Node.js

Your Express.js server needs to be able to talk to PostgreSQL. This creates a 'connection pool' — a set of reusable database connections that the server shares instead of opening a new connection for every request (which would be very slow).
Step 1: Create the database config file
Create db/pool.js and add this content:
idleTimeoutMillis: 30000, // close idle connections after 30 seconds
connectionTimeoutMillis: 2000, // fail if connection takes > 2 seconds
// Test the connection when the app starts
console.error('ERROR: Cannot connect to database:', err.message);
process.exit(1); // stop the server if DB is unreachable
DONE WHEN: No errors when this file is required by server.js — the pool connects successfully.
Write Migration 1 — Create the users table

A 'migration' is a SQL file that creates or modifies database tables. We number them (001, 002, etc.) so they always run in the right order. The users table is the foundation — every other table connects back to it.
-- The users table stores login credentials only
-- Personal info goes in user_profiles (next migration)
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS 'pgcrypto'; -- enables gen_random_uuid()
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS users (
id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
is_deleted BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE
-- Index on email so login lookups are fast
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_users_email ON users (email);
Step 3: Run the migration in TablePlus
Open TablePlus, click the SQL editor icon, paste the SQL, and click 'Run'. Then refresh the left panel.
Step 4: Verify the table was created
In TablePlus, expand your database — you should now see a 'users' table. Click it and you will see the columns.
✓ Expected result: The users table appears in TablePlus with the correct columns.
DONE WHEN: The users table is visible in TablePlus with all columns created.
Write Migration 2 — Create the user_profiles table (includes age)

The user_profiles table stores all personal and AI-analyzed information about the user. It is separate from users so the login system stays clean. The age field is stored here as a small integer (0–120).
-- Stores personal info + AI-analyzed physical attributes
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS user_profiles (
user_id UUID PRIMARY KEY REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
fitzpatrick_scale VARCHAR(5), -- I, II, III, IV, V, VI
created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
-- Auto-update the updated_at timestamp whenever a row changes
FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION update_updated_at_column();
Paste into the SQL editor and run. Refresh to verify the user_profiles table appears.
✓ Expected result: user_profiles table appears in TablePlus with the age column visible.
DONE WHEN: The user_profiles table is visible in TablePlus. Click the 'age' column to confirm it exists with type SMALLINT.
The age field uses SMALLINT (a small number, max 32767) instead of INT because ages will never be larger than 120. The CHECK constraint rejects impossible ages like -1 or 200.
Write Migration 3 — Create the wardrobe_items table

The wardrobe_items table stores every clothing item a user uploads. The JSONB type (JSON Binary) is used for ai_tags and color arrays because these are flexible, AI-generated data structures that are easier to store as JSON than as separate columns.
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS wardrobe_items (
id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
user_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
color_secondary JSONB NOT NULL DEFAULT '[]', -- array of hex strings
season JSONB NOT NULL DEFAULT '[]', -- ['Spring','Summer'] etc.
ai_tags JSONB NOT NULL DEFAULT '{}', -- full AI analysis result
cloudinary_id TEXT, -- needed to delete from Cloudinary later
is_deleted BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE,
created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_wardrobe_user ON wardrobe_items (user_id);
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_wardrobe_user_cat ON wardrobe_items (user_id, category);
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_wardrobe_last_worn ON wardrobe_items (user_id, last_worn);
Step 2: Run in TablePlus and verify
✓ Expected result: The wardrobe_items table appears with all columns and the ENUM types are created.
DONE WHEN: wardrobe_items table is visible in TablePlus with all columns including category, formality_score, and ai_tags.
Write Migration 4 — Create the outfits and saved_outfits tables
The outfits table stores every generated outfit (whether the user saved it or not). saved_outfits is a linking table that marks which outfits a user has specifically saved or favourited.
-- Migration 004: Create outfits and saved_outfits tables
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS outfits (
id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
user_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
-- Which wardrobe items make up this outfit
item_ids UUID[] NOT NULL, -- array of wardrobe_items.id
created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS saved_outfits (
user_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
outfit_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES outfits(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
is_favourite BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE,
saved_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
PRIMARY KEY (user_id, outfit_id) -- can't save the same outfit twice
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_outfits_user_date
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_outfits_user_method
Step 2: Run in TablePlus and verify
✓ Expected result: Both outfits and saved_outfits tables appear in TablePlus.
DONE WHEN: outfits and saved_outfits tables are visible in TablePlus.

These tables support the Rate My Fit feature, shopping recommendations, and AI usage tracking. The gemini_usage table is especially important — it lets you monitor how many AI API calls you are making (and their cost).
-- Migration 005: Supporting tables for external features
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS rate_my_fit_logs (
id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
user_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS shopping_recs (
id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
user_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
was_clicked BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE,
created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS gemini_usage (
id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
user_id UUID REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE SET NULL,
success BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT TRUE,
created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
Step 2: Run in TablePlus and verify
✓ Expected result: All three tables appear in TablePlus.
DONE WHEN: All 3 supporting tables are visible in TablePlus.

Right now you are running migrations manually in TablePlus. The runner script automates this — it reads all your .sql files and runs them in order automatically. This is critical for deployment (you cannot manually run SQL on a production server).
// Create a tracking table if it doesn't exist
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS schema_migrations (
applied_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
'SELECT filename FROM schema_migrations ORDER BY filename'
// Read all .sql files in db/migrations/, sorted by name
Step 2: Add the migrate script to package.json
Open package.json and add this inside the 'scripts' section:
✓ Expected result: You should see 'RUNNING: 001_create_users.sql', 'DONE', and so on for each file, ending with 'All migrations complete!'.
DONE WHEN: Running npm run migrate completes without errors and logs 'All migrations complete!'.
A seed script creates fake test data in your database so the whole team can test features without manually typing data. It creates 3 test users with full profiles and sample wardrobe items.
await client.query("DELETE FROM users WHERE email LIKE '%@test.styleai.com'");
'INSERT INTO users (email, password_hash) VALUES ($1, $2) RETURNING id',
console.log('Seed complete! Test password for all users: Password123!');
✓ Expected result: Output shows 'Created user: aisha@test.styleai.com (age: 24)' etc. In TablePlus, click the users table and run SELECT * FROM users — you should see 3 rows.
DONE WHEN: Running npm run seed creates 3 test users. Verified in TablePlus with SELECT * FROM users.
Phase 2: Build the Express Server & Authentication

Authentication is the system that lets users register, log in, and stay logged in securely. JWTs (JSON Web Tokens) are the keys your server issues — users present them with every request to prove who they are.
Build server.js (the main entry point for your Express app)
server.js is the file that starts everything. It creates the Express application, registers all middleware (security, logging, etc.), connects routes, and tells the server which port to listen on.
Step 1: Open server.js and add this content:
require('dotenv').config(); // MUST be the very first line
// helmet adds important security headers to every response
// cors controls which websites can call your API
// morgan logs every incoming request to the console
// These allow your routes to read the JSON body of requests
// Prevents abuse by limiting how many requests one IP can make
message: { error: 'Too many requests, please try again later.' }
// Simple endpoint to verify the server is alive
// We'll import and add route files here in Phase 2 & 3
// This catches any unhandled errors and returns a clean response
Step 2: Add the dev script to package.json:
✓ Expected result: Console shows 'SUCCESS: Connected to PostgreSQL database' and 'Server running on http://localhost:5000'.
Open your browser and visit http://localhost:5000/health
DONE WHEN: npm run dev starts without errors. http://localhost:5000/health returns a JSON response.
Build the AuthService (register, login, logout logic)

The AuthService is a module that handles all authentication logic. Keeping logic in a 'service' file (separate from the route handler) is good practice — routes should be thin (just handle HTTP), services should be fat (contain all the real logic).
const redis = require('../config/redis'); // we'll create this next
throw { status: 409, message: 'An account with this email already exists.' };
// 2. Hash the password (saltRounds:12 = strong but not too slow)
'INSERT INTO users (email, password_hash) VALUES ($1, $2) RETURNING id, email, created_at',
// 4. Create an empty profile row for this user
return { user: { id: user.id, email: user.email }, ...tokens };
'SELECT id, email, password_hash FROM users WHERE email = $1 AND is_deleted = FALSE',
throw { status: 401, message: 'Invalid email or password.' };
// 2. Compare submitted password with stored hash
throw { status: 401, message: 'Invalid email or password.' };
await pool.query('UPDATE users SET last_active = NOW() WHERE id = $1', [user.id]);
return { user: { id: user.id, email: user.email }, ...tokens };
// Blacklist the refresh token so it cannot be used again
throw { status: 401, message: 'Invalid or expired refresh token.' };
throw { status: 401, message: 'Refresh token has been revoked.' };
const jti = uuidv4(); // unique ID for this refresh token
// Store in Redis for 7 days (same as token expiry)
DONE WHEN: The authService.js file is created without any syntax errors (you can run node services/authService.js and see no crash).
Redis is an in-memory database used to store refresh tokens (for login sessions) and cache AI results. You need a connection to it before the AuthService will work.
// Retry connecting up to 5 times, then give up
Step 2: Add Redis connection to server.js
Add this near the top of server.js (after dotenv.config()):
Step 3: Restart the server and verify
✓ Expected result: Console now shows both 'SUCCESS: Connected to PostgreSQL database' AND 'SUCCESS: Connected to Redis'.
DONE WHEN: Server starts and logs success messages for both PostgreSQL and Redis.

The auth middleware is a gatekeeper function that runs BEFORE your route handlers. It reads the JWT token from the request, verifies it is valid, and attaches the user's ID to the request so your routes know who is making the call.
// 1. Get the token from the Authorization header
error: { message: 'No token provided. Please log in.' }
const token = authHeader.split(' ')[1]; // extract just the token part
// 2. Verify the token using our secret
req.userId = decoded.sub; // attach user ID to request
next(); // token is valid, proceed to the route
error: { message: 'Session expired. Please log in again.' }
error: { message: 'Invalid token. Please log in again.' }
DONE WHEN: The middleware file is created. You can now import and use it in route files.
Route files connect HTTP endpoints (like POST /api/auth/register) to your service functions. Each route handler validates the input, calls the service, and returns a standardized response.
// Stricter rate limit for auth routes (prevent brute force)
message: { error: 'Too many attempts. Try again in 15 minutes.' }
.isLength({ min: 8 }).withMessage('Password must be at least 8 characters')
return res.status(400).json({ success: false, error: { message: 'Validation failed', errors: errors.array() } });
return res.status(400).json({ success: false, error: { message: 'Email and password are required.' } });
res.json({ success: true, data: { message: 'Logged out successfully.' } });
return res.status(400).json({ success: false, error: { message: 'Refresh token is required.' } });
Step 2: Register the auth routes in server.js
Find the commented-out routes section in server.js and uncomment/add:
Step 3: Test registration with Thunder Client
Open Thunder Client in VS Code. Create a POST request to http://localhost:5000/api/auth/register with body:
DONE WHEN: POST /api/auth/register returns a 201 response with an accessToken and refreshToken. POST /api/auth/login with the same credentials returns a 200 with tokens.
PHASE 3 — Profile, Wardrobe & Outfit API Endpoints
Phase 3: Build the Core API Endpoints
Now that auth works, you will build the endpoints the frontend team needs to display and manage data. These endpoints are protected — they require a valid JWT access token.
Build the Profile endpoints (GET and PUT /api/profile)

These two endpoints let the frontend read and update user profile information, including the new age field. The GET endpoint joins the users and user_profiles tables to return everything in one response.
LEFT JOIN user_profiles p ON u.id = p.user_id
// Only allow safe fields to be updated (never let users change their user_id or email here)
// Build a dynamic UPDATE query from provided fields
throw { status: 400, message: 'No valid fields provided to update.' };
const query = `UPDATE user_profiles SET ${updates.join(', ')} WHERE user_id = $${paramIndex}`;
return getProfile(userId); // return the updated profile
body('age').optional().isInt({ min: 0, max: 120 }).withMessage('Age must be between 0 and 120'),
return res.status(400).json({ success: false, error: { errors: errors.array() } });
GET http://localhost:5000/api/profile with Authorization: Bearer <your_access_token> in the headers.
✓ Expected result: Returns the user's profile object including the age field.
DONE WHEN: GET /api/profile returns the user profile including the age field. PUT /api/profile with {"age": 25} updates and returns the profile with the new age.
Build the image upload middleware and Cloudinary service

Before you can build the wardrobe endpoints, you need a way to handle image uploads. Multer handles the incoming image file. Sharp resizes and compresses it. Cloudinary stores it permanently and gives you back a URL.
// Store files in memory (not disk) so Sharp can process them
cb(new Error('Only JPEG, PNG, and WEBP images are accepted.'), false);
// Configure Cloudinary with your credentials from .env
// Step 1: Process image with Sharp (resize + compress)
fit: 'inside', // never upscale, just shrink to fit within 1024x1024
.jpeg({ quality: 85 }) // convert to JPEG at 85% quality
// Step 2: Upload processed image to Cloudinary
else resolve({ url: result.secure_url, cloudinaryId: result.public_id });
// Delete an image from Cloudinary (called when user deletes a wardrobe item)
This package converts a buffer into a stream for Cloudinary:
DONE WHEN: No errors when importing these files. You can test processAndUpload by calling it with a test image buffer.
The Cloudinary credentials in .env must be real values before image upload will work. Sign up at cloudinary.com, go to your Dashboard, and copy Cloud Name, API Key, and API Secret.
These are the endpoints the frontend uses to display the user's closet, add items, edit them, and delete them. Member 3 will also call the wardrobe GET endpoint to fetch items for outfit generation.
Step 1: Create services/wardrobeService.js with the core CRUD logic:
// GET all items for a user (with optional filters)
let query = `SELECT * FROM wardrobe_items WHERE user_id = $1 AND is_deleted = FALSE`;
// Verify the item belongs to this user
'SELECT id FROM wardrobe_items WHERE id = $1 AND user_id = $2 AND is_deleted = FALSE',
if (check.rows.length === 0) throw { status: 404, message: 'Item not found.' };
if (!updates.length) throw { status: 400, message: 'No valid fields to update.' };
await pool.query(`UPDATE wardrobe_items SET ${updates.join(',')} WHERE id = $${idx}`, values);
const updated = await pool.query('SELECT * FROM wardrobe_items WHERE id = $1', [itemId]);
'UPDATE wardrobe_items SET is_deleted = TRUE WHERE id = $1 AND user_id = $2 RETURNING cloudinary_id',
if (result.rows.length === 0) throw { status: 404, message: 'Item not found.' };
// Optionally: delete the image from Cloudinary
Step 2: Create routes/wardrobe.js and register 4 routes

Create the route file following the same pattern as auth.js — GET / (list items with filter query params), POST / (upload with multer middleware), PUT /:id, DELETE /:id. Register as app.use('/api/wardrobe', require('./routes/wardrobe')) in server.js.
Step 3: Test GET /api/wardrobe (should return empty array for new user)
✓ Expected result: GET /api/wardrobe with a valid Bearer token returns { success: true, data: [] }.
DONE WHEN: All 4 wardrobe endpoints return correct responses. GET returns array, POST with an image returns the created item with image_url.

These endpoints handle saving, rating, and retrieving outfit history. The actual outfit generation logic is built by Member 3 — your job is to build the data layer: saving outfits to the database and retrieving them.
Step 1: Create routes/outfits.js with these endpoints:
// POST /api/outfits/generate → Member 3 handles the AI logic
// but the route handler lives here
// POST /api/outfits/save → save an outfit to the DB
// GET /api/outfits/history → get outfit history with filters
// PUT /api/outfits/:id/rate → update the rating on an outfit
const { item_ids, generation_method, mood_tag, rating, notes, worn_date } = req.body;
return res.status(400).json({ success: false, error: { message: 'item_ids array is required.' } });
INSERT INTO outfits (user_id, item_ids, generation_method, mood_tag, rating, notes, worn_date)
`, [req.userId, item_ids, generation_method || 'prompt', mood_tag, rating, notes, worn_date]);
// GET /api/outfits/history (with optional date range and pagination)
const { from, to, limit = 20, offset = 0 } = req.query;
let query = 'SELECT * FROM outfits WHERE user_id = $1';
query += ` ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT $${idx++} OFFSET $${idx++}`;
DONE WHEN: POST /api/outfits/save saves an outfit and returns it. GET /api/outfits/history returns an array (empty at first).
A backend that works is not enough — it needs to be secure, observable (you can see what's happening), and fast. This phase adds the layers that take your API from 'it works' to 'production ready'.

console.log is fine for development but terrible for production. Winston creates structured log files with log levels (info, warning, error), timestamps, and consistent formatting. When something breaks in production, logs are how you find out what happened.
winston.format.json() // structured JSON logs for production
winston.format.simple() // easier to read in development
// In production, also write to files
Step 2: Replace console.log in your service files
Wherever you have console.error or console.log in your service files, import and use the logger instead:
logger.error('Database error', { error: err.message, stack: err.stack });
DONE WHEN: The server starts and logs are formatted with timestamps. An info log and an error log appear in the console.
Add Redis caching for AI-generated outfit suggestions

Every time a user generates an outfit, your server calls the Gemini API. This is slow (2–4 seconds) and uses API credits. If the user generates an outfit with the same mood and the same wardrobe within 6 hours, we can return the cached result instantly instead of calling Gemini again.
// Create a unique cache key based on user + mood + their wardrobe content
// Hash the wardrobe so the key changes if the wardrobe changes
async function cacheOutfits(userId, moodTag, wardrobeItems, outfits) {
// Invalidate outfit cache when wardrobe changes
In wardrobeService.js, after adding, updating, or deleting an item, call:
DONE WHEN: The cache service file is created with no errors. Member 3 will integrate getCachedOutfits/cacheOutfits into the outfit generation endpoint.
AI endpoints are expensive. If someone calls /api/outfits/generate 100 times per minute, your Gemini API bill will spike. A tighter rate limit on AI-specific endpoints protects your usage quota.
Step 1: Add this tighter rate limiter to your outfit generation route:
// Only 10 outfit generations per 15 minutes per IP
error: { message: 'AI generation limit reached. Please wait 15 minutes.' }
// Apply to the generate endpoint only
router.post('/generate', authenticate, aiLimiter, async (req, res, next) => {
DONE WHEN: Calling /api/outfits/generate more than 10 times in quick succession returns a 429 Too Many Requests response.
Phase 5: Deploy Your Backend to Production
Deployment means making your backend accessible on the internet so the frontend (on Vercel) can call it. Railway is a beginner-friendly hosting platform that makes this straightforward.
Prepare for deployment — add a start script and environment check

Before deploying, you need to make sure your app starts correctly in production (where NODE_ENV=production) and that it fails loudly if any required environment variable is missing — rather than silently running broken.
Step 1: Create config/env.js (validates all required env vars on startup):
process.exit(1); // crash immediately with a clear message
Step 3: Add 'engines' to package.json (tells Railway which Node version to use):
DONE WHEN: Running NODE_ENV=production npm start with a missing env var logs the FATAL error and exits. With all vars present, it starts normally.
Railway is a cloud platform that runs your Node.js server and provides managed PostgreSQL and Redis. It connects directly to your GitHub repo and auto-deploys when you push to the main branch.
Go to https://railway.app — sign up with your GitHub account.
Click 'New Project' → 'Deploy from GitHub repo' → select the styleai repo → select the backend subfolder if prompted.
In your Railway project, click '+ New' → 'Database' → 'Add PostgreSQL'. Railway creates a managed PostgreSQL instance and automatically sets DATABASE_URL in your environment.
Click '+ New' → 'Database' → 'Add Redis'. Railway sets REDIS_URL automatically.
Step 5: Add all other environment variables
Click on your backend service → 'Variables' tab → add each variable from your .env file one by one (JWT_SECRET, CLOUDINARY keys, GEMINI_API_KEY, FRONTEND_URL, etc.).
In Railway service settings, set Start Command to: npm start
In Railway, go to your backend service → open a terminal shell → run:
✓ Expected result: All 5 migration files run successfully on the production database.
In Railway, go to your service → Settings → Domains → Generate domain. Your API is now live at something like https://styleai-backend.up.railway.app
✓ Expected result: Visiting https://your-railway-url.up.railway.app/health in a browser returns { "status": "ok" }.
DONE WHEN: The /health endpoint returns {status: ok} on your live Railway URL. Share this URL with Member 1 and Member 2 so they can update VITE_API_URL in the frontend.
Everything in Phases 0–5 should be done BEFORE the hackathon. This phase is a final pre-flight checklist and your personal plan for the 24 hours.
Pre-hackathon checklist — everything to verify the night before
Arriving at the hackathon with a broken environment is a disaster. Run through this entire checklist the night before.
npm run dev starts the server with no errors locally
✓ Expected result: Console shows PostgreSQL, Redis, and 'Server running' messages.
npm run migrate runs all 5 migrations cleanly on local DB
✓ Expected result: Logs show all 5 files as 'DONE'.
npm run seed creates 3 test users
✓ Expected result: TablePlus shows 3 rows in the users table.
✓ Expected result: Returns 201 with accessToken and refreshToken.
POST /api/auth/login works with the seeded test user
GET /api/profile with a valid Bearer token returns user data including age
✓ Expected result: Returns profile object with age field.
POST /api/wardrobe/items with an image file creates a new wardrobe item with Cloudinary URL
✓ Expected result: Returns 201 with image_url pointing to cloudinary.com.
Railway production URL /health returns { status: ok }
Production DB migrations have run (check via TablePlus connected to Railway DB)
✓ Expected result: All 7 tables exist in production.
All environment variables are set in Railway (double-check every single one)
✓ Expected result: Railway Variables tab shows all required keys.
DONE WHEN: All 10 checks pass. You are ready for the hackathon.
During the hackathon, work in this order. Do not jump ahead — each block unblocks the next team member.
docker compose up -d locally. Confirm Railway is live. Share production URL with team. Confirm Member 3 can call your /health endpoint from their machine.
SSH into Railway and run npm run migrate on production. Confirm all tables exist. Run npm run seed on production to create the demo account.
Deploy the auth routes to production. Test register + login on the live URL with Postman. Share the test account credentials with the whole team.
Deploy profile routes. Test GET and PUT /api/profile including age. Confirm Member 2 can call it from the frontend.
Deploy wardrobe routes. Test image upload to Cloudinary on the live server. Confirm Member 1 can display items from the API.
Step 6: Hour 6–8: Outfit history endpoints live
Deploy outfit save + history + rating endpoints. Confirm Member 2 can save and retrieve outfits.
Member 3 is building outfit generation endpoints. Help them with any database query issues. Add any missing DB queries they need.
Help deploy Rate My Fit and Shopping recommendation endpoints as Member 3 finishes them. Fix any database-level bugs that surface.
Add Redis caching calls. Check Railway logs for any errors. Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on slow queries in TablePlus.
Monitor production logs during full-team QA. Fix any backend errors immediately. Verify demo account has a full wardrobe.
No new DB schema changes. Monitor Railway logs. Ensure the demo account is perfect. Confirm all endpoints return correct status codes.
DONE WHEN: At Hour 24, all backend endpoints are live, stable, and returning correct responses on the production Railway URL.
Start backend server with auto-restart on save
Create test users and sample data
Start PostgreSQL and Redis in background
See PostgreSQL logs if connection fails
All Your API Endpoints at a Glance
Get a new access token using refresh token
Upload selfie → Gemini analysis → update profile
Upload a new clothing item (image + metadata)
Common Errors & How to Fix Them
PostgreSQL is not running. Run: docker compose up -d
Redis is not running. Run: docker compose up -d
You have not created config/redis.js yet — see Task 20
JWT_SECRET in .env does not match the one used to sign the token — restart the server after changing .env
Uploaded image is over 5MB. Tell the user to upload a smaller image.
duplicate key value violates unique constraint
Trying to register an email that already exists — return 409 Conflict to the user
invalid input value for enum item_category
Frontend sent a category value not in the ENUM list — check the exact allowed values in migration 003
Migrations have not been run. Run: npm run migrate
StyleAI | Member 4 — Major Backend Guide | Web App v1.0 | Confidential
